text:
North Koreado have athletes competing at theOlympicsinParisbut viewers may not immediately recognise their country code at the Games.

The secretive state has sent 16 athletes to compete in seven different sports in the French capital thissummer.

North Korea are already in the medals as Ri Jong-sik and Kim Kum-yong won silver in the table tennis mixed doubles on Tuesday.

They will be aiming to add to their medal tally on Wednesday morning as Kim Mi-rae and Jin Mi-jo are competing in the women’s 10m synchronised platform diving.

North Korea compete at the Olympics under the country code PRK.

That is a shortened version of the nation’s full name – Democratic People’s Republic of Korea.

Given the lack of knowledge over the athletes sent to the Olympics by North Korea, there is an added level of intrigue whenever they compete.

North Korea’s artistic gymnast, An Chang-Ok, was filmed in the Olympic Village this week trading pins with other athletes.

An Chang-Ok trading pins in the Olympic village. Glad to see her having fun!pic.twitter.com/d8ete69JDB

United States tennis legend Serena Williams is a self-proclaimed ‘first-class pin collector’, as she started trading badges with other athletes at her first Olympics in Sydney in 2000.

Williams finally got her hands on a North Korea pin at Rio 2016 and said last week: ‘I would never, ever, ever trade that.’

Elsewhere, Olympics officials were forced to apologise before the Games kicked off in Parisafter South Korean athletes were announced as as North Korean during the opening ceremony on Friday.

As South Korean athletes travelled along the River Seine on a boat they were announced as the Democratic People’s Republic of Korea.

A spokesperson for the International Olympic Committee (IOC), said: ‘An operational mistake was made. We can only apologise, in an evening of so many moving parts, that this mistake was made.’

title: What is PRK at the Olympics? North Korea country code explained | Metro News
description: North Korea do have athletes competing at the Olympics in Paris but viewers may not immediately recognise their country code at the Games.
